title: Sustaining the local color of a global city
data_sources: historical photographs, paintings, postcards (National Museum of Singapore, NLB)

data_scope.time_span: over 40 years before and after conservation
data_scope.unreported: image resolution, exact count of historical/archival images, and specific CV model versions are not stated in the main text

core_conclusion: A chromatic reconstruction of 3,103 inhabited heritage shophouses in Singapore over 40+ years shows local color is not constant but continuously re-inscribed through historicization, ethnicization and commercialization, manifesting across five color dimensions (dominant colors, complexity, harmony, saturation, value). Color should be treated as a living part of the city's sociocultural ecology rather than a fixed material attribute to be preserved in isolation.
why_it_matters: Challenges the assumption that heritage palettes are static and should be frozen; operationalizes intangible cultural change into measurable color metrics; fuses computational color extraction with archival and fieldwork sources; reframes conservation as managing living ecology rather than embalming the past.
why_nature: Couples physical urban fabric (color) with demography, migration and meaning; integrates machine learning with archival/field methods; offers a large longitudinal before/after natural experiment around a real policy intervention; delivers a policy-translatable reframing of heritage conservation.
added_value_to_my_research: Port the 5-dimension color pipeline to global street-view as a reusable visual-environment covariate layer
Couple chromatic change to census/migration data via spatiotemporal MGWR/GWR for space-human coupling
Add explainable-ML attribution (SHAP) over color outcomes -> template for my spatial-prediction models
Model color harmony as a graph-Laplacian smoothness signal -> physics-informed graph learning use case
Archive provenance-and-uncertainty protocol -> reusable for FAIR multi-source data fusion
